emergent_themes.description: Students actively avoid using shared kitchen spaces when flatmates are present, driving delivery orders as an alternative to cooking
Food viewed primarily as fuel - prioritizing portability, speed, and efficiency over social or pleasurable dining experiences
Clear evolution from high first-year ordering (freedom-driven) to low final-year ordering (cost-conscious and skilled)
Minimum order thresholds create significant barriers for solo ordering, often preventing orders entirely

coding_summary: P005 represents a highly evolved student user who has 'matured away' from delivery dependency. Her food approach is distinctly functional - prioritizing portability, efficiency, and cost-effectiveness over social dining. Key patterns include: dramatic reduction in ordering from first year to final year, strong cost-benefit analysis preventing most solo orders, shared kitchen avoidance driving some delivery decisions, and group ordering with her brother to overcome minimum spend barriers. She demonstrates sophisticated understanding of platform differences and subscription benefits, but remains primarily cost-driven in her decisions.

coverage_gaps.reason: Participant doesn't express stigma around solo ordering - her barriers are purely cost-based rather than social stigma
